summary: Yegge argues that the fundamental problem with software is that we build 'dead' systems requiring constant reboots, rather than 'living' systems that can grow and evolve while running. He identifies key properties of great software—command shells, advice systems, extension languages, plugin architectures, and introspection—all deriving from the principle that systems should never need to reboot. He critiques static type systems as tools for building inflexible 'hardware' rather than adaptable software, and suggests that truly great software exhibits an ineffable 'Quality Without a Name' (QWAN) that emerges from organic growth rather than rigid design.
key_points: The most important principle in software design is that systems should never reboot—if designed this way, they will eventually become systems that can live forever
Great software systems share essential features: command shells, advice/hook systems, extension languages, plugin architectures, and deep introspection capabilities
Static type systems are for building 'hardware'—deterministic, inflexible layers that trade flexibility for performance—while dynamic languages enable building true 'software' that can grow
Most programmers build 'marionettes' (dead, controllable systems) rather than 'real children' (living, evolving software) because living software is scary and harder to manage
Software can possess consciousness on a gradient, and rebooting a sufficiently complex system may eventually become ethically equivalent to killing it
money_quotes: I think most software is crap. Well, that's not quite right. It's fairer to say that I think all software is crap.
Systems should never reboot. If you design a system so that it never needs to reboot, then you will eventually, even if it's by a very roundabout path, arrive at a system that will live forever.
Living software is a little scary. It's no surprise that most programmers prefer writing marionettes instead of real people; marionettes are a lot easier to manage.
There's nothing wrong with static type systems. You just have to realize that when you use them, you're building hardware, not software.
I think of most of the software we build today as being like setting up dominoes. It's Domino Design.
The reason H-M isn't used in the industry, folks, is that it doesn't have any holes.
We will never be able to make real progress in computing and language design in our industry until C syntax is wholly eradicated: a task that could take fifty years.
If you treat your software like a living thing, then eventually it just might become one.
key_insight: The best software systems are 'living' rather than 'dead'—designed to grow and evolve without rebooting—and this single principle drives all other qualities that make software great.
